speaker: interviewer
text: Three, an interview with Olga Drucker in Merrick, New York on March 25, 1996. Let's continue from where we left off. You're in Wellingborough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your schooling experience and how you would spend your days when you were not in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was not in school? Well, that would be weekends-- or, you mean, like, after school? Well, there was a lot of-- the emphasis was on religion. And there were a lot of meetings that I went to. And my, my auntie-- in fact, her name is not-- I didn't call her by her real name in my book. But it's-- her real name was Rhoda, like your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: How interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she was the daughter of missionaries. She had, herself, although she was English, had been born in China. And so she was very-- this was fundamentalist Baptist. This is not funny. And I went to a lot of services and meetings. And I just threw myself into it-- that and my music. And the school, as I said before, just went slipping down, down, down. But that was the life. I, I played with the other girls. And in fact, we played in the victory garden that this family had. And I remember that was a lot of fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm trying to remember. There was a little air raid shelter underneath the-- well, it was a space underneath the stairs that a lot of houses have to this day a little broom closet or whatever. That was our air raid shelter. And we were-- trained ourselves to get up, if we had to, in the middle of the night and run down there. There was a mattress on the floor. And we would just continue sleeping.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we learned to recognize by the sounds of the planes going overhead whether they were British or German. And once in awhile, there would be a bomb that, perhaps accidentally, fell on Wellingborough, and perhaps not so accidentally. But there wasn't that much activity, bombing activity, in Wellingborough. It was in the Midlands. It was further away from the coast. And they didn't get that far. We did, towards the end of the war, see the planes going over for D-day. We saw all those planes going over in formation, and the gliders that, that were being towed. We saw all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think was happening when you saw all this activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew something was up, you know. We'd-- well, we were cheering all the way, of course. We also saw-- Coventry isn't terribly far from Wellingborough. And we did see the fires that occurred in Coventry when that was being bombed. So [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your brother during this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London-- oh, well, in London, except at the beginning of the war-- you just jogged my memory again-- German, Germans living in England were considered enemy aliens, whether they were German-- I mean non-Jewish German or Jewish German-- didn't make any difference. War is crazy. So my brother, being of the right age, was sent away as an enemy alien to the Isle of Man, interned in the, the Isle of Man, for some time. He was a-- they released him again after they realized that, that they had made a mistake, you know, after a few months, I guess. And this was not the same as a concentration camp. It just meant barbed wire all around. But other than that, they led a fairly normal life. He couldn't work, of course, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your life went on?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my life went on. I wrote my letters--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your schooling was not wonderful. Did you stay in school till you were able to finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't finish. Legally, in England, you can leave school when you're 16 years old. I wasn't done with it yet, but at 16 I was doing so poorly that I lost heart. By that time, I was living with another family also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not clear about that. The theory that-- what was told to me was that it was closer to the school, which it was. And in any case, the Refugee Committee found out about the religious activity that was going on. Perhaps that's true. And possibly, there were no other Jews in all of Wellingborough, so that they assigned me to this family. Or this family was found for me, whatever. And there, before I quit school, I had missed-- I had to miss a lot of my school and my exams and everything, because the woman there was in poor health and expected me to take care of her-- stay home from school and take care of her. So I did that. You know, again, I wasn't unhappy there. I wasn't unhappy. I don't remember, really-- oh, maybe I didn't even know that I was unhappy, because I was a pretty lonely kid. Maybe I was unhappier than I've ever admitted to. But-- and maybe I didn't find that out until I moved again, when I-- once I quit school, and I found a job. I wanted to be a children's nurse. And I wanted--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why you wanted to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I wanted to look after kids. I always liked kids. And I thought that would be a nice thing for me to train to do. But there was no money for me to go to any schooling for that. I don't know where I would have gone, in any case. My, my-- I know my parents didn't want me to leave school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in the United States at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were now in the United States. And-- but nobody could tell me anything. I was 16 years old, you know? And, but-- so the next best thing that came along was this wonderful woman who lived near the first family in Wellingborough that I had lived with, who had a house full of kids. She had, at that time, five children. And she needed a mother's helper. She had just had twins. She desperately needed some help.

speaker: interviewer
text: She really needed a mother's helper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I jumped at the chance. I thought that would be wonderful. And it, and it was wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you met her prior to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never had. She, she stopped by-- she, the way we met was she-- I was visiting at this at Auntie Rhoda. And Mrs. Wooley was outside-- we were outside on the street. And they started to talk. And, and one thing led to another. She had this baby carriage with her. And she said, look in there. They're my twins. And I just fell in love with them. And she said she needed somebody to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Wooley was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she knows that you were Jewish and that you were a refugee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did. Yes, she did. I think everybody knew that. But, you know, I don't know what it meant to them. But in any case, Mrs. Wooley-- I, I have now, oh, for years already after I grew up, we called each other-- I call her by her first name, Mariana.

speaker: interviewee
text: And where was I? Oh yeah, she, she didn't care whether I went to church or didn't go to church. There was no synagogue for me to go to. And she knew that it probably didn't matter to me. So I was a pretty much left alone. Did you live in with this family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daily life like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, there I, I, I did all of the-- a lot of housework. There was another nanny there. And she and I did a lot of the housework and looked after the children. Those that went to school, we took to sch-- walked to the school and took the baby carriages with us. And I had a lot of work to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Mr. Wooley like? Was he at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was at home, yes. He was a principal of a boys' school at that time. Later on, he resigned from that. And in England, somehow the theological is tied into the educational. So he became a minister in the Church of England.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was not in the military at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, he was a father of, what did I say, five, six? I have to count them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year-- just a year. And it was really the happiest year of my life. Because they made me feel like part of them. They-- that was a happy family. They didn't expect me to be grateful and say thank you for everything. And they didn't-- it's not so much that they left me alone. Because I knew that, that I was being supervised. But they were just so-- such a happy family. And they still are. And we still see each other. And the kids, of course, are now all grown up. And some of them are grandparents themselves, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: They remembered those years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes, they do. One lives in Canada. And the others is all in England. And we see each other fairly often.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they've become extended family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Extended family, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in touch with your parents during this period?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, once they got to America, yeah, then we wrote again. The letters still took a long time. And they had to be-- get past the censors. Every letter that we got was-- had been opened by the censor, you know. And some of them looked, perhaps, water damaged. But yes, we could communicate. And they had it pretty rough in America. They had to start from scratch. And, and they did. And they never talked about the past. They, they called that the dark days. And they wouldn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even years later, after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not ever, and i couldn't ask. I just couldn't ask.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you had stayed with the Wooley family for a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened at the end of that year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then-- my parents, from the day that they arrived in New York, were trying to get my visa to join them, and my brother also. And that took some more doing. There was still a war going on. And they finally got my visa just before the end of the war. I was in England exactly six years, almost to the day. In March, '45, is when I left. And Mrs. Wooley came with me, took the train with me from Wellingborough to Swansea in Wales, and saw me onto the ship-- I can't remember the name of the ship-- which went up to Halifax, Nova Scotia. And then from Halifax, it was another three days' train journey down to Grand Central Station in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that journey from Swansea to Halifax? Who was on board ship with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some-- I, I hooked up with some other kids my age who were with different circumstances, none of them like me. I-- there was one war bride. I can't remember. There was one, there was one son of a French diplomat that we all flirted with, or whatever. And, and you know, we just stuck together and made our own good time. I was a bit sea sick. I always am on a ship, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that ship part of a convoy, or was it travel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was. It was part of a convoy. It took 13 days to cross. We zigzagged across the Atlantic Ocean. And you go-- in a convoy, you go as fast as the slowest vessel, after all, because we all had to stick together. And there were torpedo, torpedo drills, and I think one torpedo. I'm never sure whether it was the real thing or not. It might have been. They didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if any ships were lost?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't, I don't believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you got to Halifax.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, from there, you traveled by train.

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who traveled on the train with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most-- I think, the other kids. But I was alone, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was now 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were a young adult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Although, how adult I was, I don't know. I don't think I grew up till I was maybe 50, so. What was that trip like from Halifax to Grand Central Station, New York? How did you feel? I was very apprehensive, actually. Although, we were fooling around a lot-- you know, kidding around a lot, and playing, playing cards with each other, playing games. But I was apprehensive, because I hadn't seen my parents now for six years. I had forgotten most of my German. And I didn't know whether they'd recognize me, although they'd seen pictures of me, but and, and I was right. Because in their minds, I was still that 11-year-old that they left, that, that had left them, six years earlier. And they didn't recognize me right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you connect?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I recognized them, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was Grand Central Station, New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grand Central Station, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that we went on the subway and the-- to Kew Gardens, which is where they lived now. And they've started out on the Upper West Side. And then, when they knew I was coming, they found an apartment in Kew Gardens. And the subway was so different from the London subway. If you've been to London, you know what I mean. And even in those days, it wasn't particularly clean. And it was-- like, so many people and so, so many buildings. I had never seen anything like it. And later, when my uncle who was here, took me around a little bit and showed me the stores and I saw fresh oranges in the shops. And I just couldn't get over it. Because food was really rationed in England. We didn't have any of that. And it was all very exciting and a bit overwhelming. And I went back to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forest Hills High-- everybody said, oh, you must get into high school. In those days, a high school diploma meant much more than it does today, I think. It was-- probably is what a college degree means. So I had to do that. And I squeaked by. I had to miss-- I had to add another year, because I didn't know American history. But I-- finally, I got out. Played in the school orchestra in Forest Hills High, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What instrument did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cello. I, I got my cello back. And how they ever let me play, I don't know. Because I couldn't have played very well. But they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make close friends during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, yes. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there others in a similar situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sure. Kew Gardens is another enclave, you know, just like Washington Heights was. And I did. I have, in fact, the two girls that I-- there were three girls that I was friendly with. And I always felt like I crashed into their party, because they had known each other much longer. But they took me in, so I was OK. And two of those, I have-- I see sometimes even now, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: After you finished high school, what did you do next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became a dental assistant. I saw-- on the day of graduation, I had no idea what I was going to do. And there was a notice on the wall in my home room that said there was a course being offered for dental assisting, six weeks course. And I signed up for it. And on 42nd Street, the school of-- I'm blocking, I can't remember the name of it. But anyway, so I took that course. And I got a job as a dental assistant. I did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you work in that field?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four years, before I was married, and the beginning of our marriage. And then, again, after my third child was born, I did another year of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met him in Kew Gardens. And I don't know if he told you that story. I was going out with a boy, also from, from Germany-- happened to be from Berlin. And his name was Bob [? Frahm. ?] And he was a friend of Rolf's from Germany. One day-- this was after Bob and I had already broken up, I was-- there was an ice cream store in Kew Gardens, a drug store in Kew Gardens opening up. And they were giving away free ice cream. And I was there with a girlfriend. And this was Helen, who had been in Forest Hills High with me. And she had gone out-- it sounds like a soap opera. She had gone out with Rolf. And Rolf was at the same place with Bob, with whom I had gone out with before. And Rolf and I met. And he walked me home, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the beginning of a courtship.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the beginning of a long courtship.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1950, two years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children. There's Jane, who was born in '52. And she lives in Los Angeles. And there's Robert, who lives in Seattle, who was born in '55. And there's Alice, who was born in '60-- in '59. And she's in partly Florida and partly in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alice is married to Tom. And they have a child, whose name is Andrew. He's now three years old. And he's the joy of our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you made any decisions during the course of your lifetime, based on your experience as a child on the Kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a lot, I supp-- or, yes. First of all, I am always trying to, to be good to kids and certainly favor foster children in particular. For a while, we had a fo-- not, not physically in the house-- but we sponsored a foster child by, you know, by sending them clothing and toys and stuff. I think that now, I, I have a great need to talk about this experience, and, and especially to children. I do go into schools when I'm asked to and tell them about my experience. Because I, I want them to, to learn tolerance and be, be nice to each other, and, and know what can happen if, if one group hates another group. That can't be. And while I'm at it, I, I think I'm going to take the opportunity right now to thank Steven Spielberg for this opportunity that he's given me and so many thousands of others to tell our story. It's, it's the most important thing that I can think of. And that's the big-- and that's why I wrote a book about my experience. It's called And it's, it's a scholastic selection. It's going around in the schools and American libraries and anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you say earlier that your-- this book is also being published in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany and in Holland, and I'm going to Germany-- Rolf is coming with me-- this summer on a book tour. And I'll be talking to German kids at that point. And that is really an opportunity I never dreamed I would have. And it's-- I'm very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very, very much for sharing your story and your testimony with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're entirely welcome. In this picture, which is taken between the two posts of a gymnastics set that I had in Stuttgart in our backyard-- and flanking on either side are my, my father on the left and my mother on the right. And in the middle are my grandparents, my maternal grandparents. And I'm the little girl in the front. This was probably taken around 1935. My grandfather died of natural causes in 1936. And my grandmother was taken to Theresienstadt. And she died there around 1943 or '44 That's it for that. This was my house that my parents had built in 19-- we moved into it in 1933, in Stuttgart. There's a house similar to it on the left side, which belonged to my uncle's family, my father's brother. The one on the other side was-- belonged to a family by the name of [? Guombo, ?] who did not survive the Holocaust. They died in a concentration camp. And my parents lived in that house until they left in 1941. It was a one-family house. [? CREW: ?] [INAUDIBLE] The, the, the car was the company car, belonged to the book publishing company that my uncle who was driving around. This is the house that I describe in my book called I open up with that. This is my-- one of my school class pictures in Stuttgart. The teacher in the middle, her name was Fraulein [? Brumer. ?] And you can always tell which one is me, because I wear these big bows in my head. And I'm usually the smallest one in the class anyhow. So I'm the one in the fr-- all the way on the left in the front row. I remember very few of the girls' names. There's another kid with a bow, second from the left from Fraulein [? Brumer. ?] I think that was [? Marianne ?] [? Mariana, ?] the one who crossed the street when she was coming towards me and didn't talk to me, because she had been told by her parents that she shouldn't talk to Jewish kids. That's all I can talk about that one. They took a picture like that every year. In this picture, which I don't personally remember seeing, but my parents had brought out a number of photographs from Germany-- and obviously, this is a parade in Germany, possibly in Stuttgart. I'm pretty sure it was in Stuttgart. And the man in the middle, who has a different hat on, is Hindenburg, who was president in Germany before Hitler. And these, of course, are all Nazi soldiers. This is a picture of my brother, Herbert, whose name was Hans at the time. And it was taken in England in the spring of 1939, which would be just about out of time when I arrived in England myself. So this is what he looked like when he picked me up in London off the boat from Germany. Mama and papa-- these were the pictures that I had of my parents on my dresser all the time I was in England. And you can see-- these were pictures taken in Germany before they left. And you can see how actually serious, and rather sad, my father looks. He had been in Dachau at that point. And my mother, well, she was always hoping for the best. We have here my father's passport, issued to him by the Germans as-- for leaving Germany. And it says here, Dr. Richard Lenk, and that he was a book publisher, born in Stuttgart on the 11th of October, 1880. He lived in Stuttgart. He was of medium frame, bodily farm, oval face, blue eyes, blond hair, and no distinguishing features.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wrote a poem about my father many years later, when I was writing a lot of poems about the Holocaust at the time. This one I entitled, 'Dachau, 'Tell me, Daddy, how was it when you sat in that train? The one that took you to-- but you didn't know where it was taking you. When at last, they let you out, your lips were sealed with threat of death. You never told me. Did you ever whisper into her ears, in the dark of night, in the safety of her arms? Only, where could one be sure? Or was that train ride a scorching memory inside the safe caverns of your skull for the rest of your life, leaving a dead patch of leathery bone on your brain, like a cigarette burn on a parchment lamp shade?'
